On the Nature of Free Association

Stanley Rosner Ph.D.

Stanley Rosner

Stanley Rosner, Ph.D.
110 South Avenue
New Canaan, Conn. 06840

Stanley Rosner  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to delineate some of the phenomena involved in free association and to identify some of the known cognitive processes that might underlie these phenomena. My purpose has been to further the rapprochement between cognitive and psychoanalytic psychologies.

APPARENTLY IT HAS BECOME MORE FREQUENT in recent years for a candidate to openly object to the fact that his analyst sends reports about his analysis to the education committee. It is not difficult for the analyst to understand the candidate's fear of exposure, censure, and disaccreditation or to discern the narcissistic resistances and the inhibited exhibitionism that play significant roles in the determination of such objections. If the analyst has not, in fact, abused his reportorial function, analytic work with such complaints and the demand that the analyst should not so report can be exceedingly fruitful.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editor's Note

Harold Blum M.D.

Harold Blum

Harold Blum  

THE SPECIAL nature and tasks of training analysis are fundamental not only to psychoanalytic education, but, ultimately, to the entire field of psychoanalysis. The training analytic situation has many complexities and potential complications. The dilemma of the training analyst, with his multiple functions and responsibilities, is here exemplified in the controversy over reporting and possible related involvement in the candidate's progression.

Views concerning the value, necessity, and effects of these reports differ. The controversy envelops basic issues of analytic confidentiality, neutrality, and trust as well as questions of maintaining standards, protecting the profession and patients, sharing training responsibility, and the optimal facilitation and integration of therapy and education.

Reporting is a significant issue in our growing understanding of the problems of psychoanalytic training and of the possible "institutionalization" of analysis in the training situation. The controversy about reporting indicates the need for critical inquiry and open discourse. The following paper, an extended discussion of "The Nonreporting Training Analyst, the Analysis, and the Institute," suggests additional dimensions and alternative considerations.

William Pieper  

This Panel was concerned with the issue of analyzability of child patients. Marjorie Harley, the Chairman, explained that the discussions would be based upon Anna Freud's developmental profile for the assessment of childhood health and pathology. Harley outlined the independent variables that determine the analyzability of any given child—i.e., the degree of phase-appropriate psychic structure, and the role of the parent.

In discussing the variable of ego structure, Harley specified what could be termed an "analyzability ratio" that has to be considered: the numerator being the degree of internalized (intersystemic) conflict, the denominator, the degree of psychic structural defect. She believed that the terms of this analyzability ratio should not be viewed in isolation from each other: "The partial deficiencies in ego development may, in some cases, increase the potential for (and become entangled with) later severe neurotic conflict, and this in turn may lessen the potential for at least a partial reversability of the primary ego defects. In addition, the texture and patterning of the later neurotic conflicts are inevitably influenced by these developmental defects."

Harley stated that the view of psychic development has gained added dimension because of our increased knowledge of early ego development and the vital role of the aggressive drive, not only in intersystemic conflicts, but also in augmenting or impeding ego growth and expansion. In this context, she cited especially Mahler's empirically based theory of early ego development.

Held at the Fall Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December, 1972, Chairman: Marjorie Harley, Ph.D.

Submitted February 28, 1973

[[RunningHead]]

When Friends Fall Out": Developmental Interference with the Function of Some Imaginary Companions

Ronald Benson M.D. and David Pryor Ph.D.

David Pryor

Ronald M. Benson, M.D.
David B. Pryor, Ph.D.
Children's Psychiatric Hospital
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48104

David Pryor  

SUMMARY

Two cases are presented of children who abruptly gave up their imaginary companions after adults in their environment ascribed a meaning to these companions which differed from the child's needs. We focus on a developmental role played by these children's imaginary companions and on the external interventions which interfered with that developmental role. We emphasize the imaginary companion's role in the developmental line of the children's narcissism and relate it to similar functions of the typical transitional object.

Theodore L. Dorpat, M.D.
2271 N.E. 51 Street

Theodore Dorpat  

In his introductory remarks to the panel, the Chairman, Herbert J. Schlesinger, observed that psychoanalysis has held rather mixed attitudes toward research. Rightly regarding itself to be a science, it has yet been defensive about its method, especially when challenged about the scientific limitations of the private, secluded situation in which its findings are obtained, a situation that admits of no outside observers and seems essentially nonreplicable.

Although psychoanalysis, particularly in recent years, has openly encouraged research in many areas, it has rarely encouraged research into the psychoanalytic situation itself. While a negative climate is perhaps partially responsible for the paucity of research into the psychoanalytic process, a more serious impediment is the fact that such research is fearfully difficult to do. When one imposes a research purpose upon a therapeutic analysis and intrudes into the psychoanalytic situation in pursuit of the research, the complexities of both the research and the therapy mount exponentially.

Schlesinger discussed the requirements incumbent on the analyst who would conduct research on the psychoanalytic process. He must first assure that a psychoanalysis is, in fact, taking place, and then, that the research procedures introduced do not significantly distort the psychoanalytic process.

Another requirement is that the analytic data must be "public," i.e., available for independent study by others than the one who gathered them.

Held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Dallas, April—May, 1972.

Submitted August 21, 1972

Comments on Movement Therapy as a Psychotherapeutic Tool

Judith Kestenberg M.D.

Judith Kestenberg

Judith S. Kestenberg, M.D.
30 Soundview Lane
Sands Point, New York 11050

Judith Kestenberg  

In a lively presentation, Siegel tries to link her technique of movement therapy with psychoanalytic theory. This is by no means an easy task, especially insofar as psychoanalytic theory is based on metapsychology and psychoanalytic therapy on a day-by-day, minute-by-minute, metapsychological assessment of the patient's productions and the analyst's technique. Psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapies and the parapsychiatric techniques of reeducation, dance-art-and music therapies are not usually bound by a comparable theoretical framework and a comparable assessment.

Siegel's aims are to use motor therapy as an ego-building tool to help the patient desomatize and aid him in the restructuring of the body image, rather than disorganize him by seductive libidinization and aggressivization of the body. To evaluate how close she comes to these ideals, we need to assess not only the patient but also the technical procedures as applied to the particular individual. As analysts, we make such assessments primarily from our patients' and our own verbal productions, but we also note and understand intuitively motor expressions and intonations. This requires a talent for an easy transposition of clinical manifestations to psychoanalytic theorizations.

The ideas expressed in this commentary are mine only, but they were developed on the basis of discussions among Dr. H. Marcus, Dr. J. Berlowe and myself, all of the Movement Study Group of Child Development Research, Sands Point, N.Y. This group, composed also of Dr. Esther Robbins, Mrs. Arnhilt Buelte, and Mrs. Martha Soodak, has devised a movement profile from which a developmental, metapsychological assessment can be made. The validity of various parts of the profile is being tested on studies from Kibbutzim and from the Social Center for Parents and Children, Port Washington, N.Y.

Submitted January 24, 1973

Fifth Avenue and 100 St.
New York, New York 10029

Stuart Feder  

SUMMARY

Using the psychoanalytic method as an investigative technique, four cases are presented to show the range of intrapsychic factors motivating individuals to join experiential groups. The details of

865

group choice were congruent with themes developed in the analyses. Conscious and unconscious factors operating in the service of both wish and defense are summarized and discussed. The problem of acting out is considered, with specific reference to the transference and to environmental, extrapsychic forces which may induce, facilitate, and sanction acting-out behavior.

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

THE EFFECT OF STRESS ON DREAMS. By Louis Breger, Ian Hunter & Ron W. Lane. Psychological Issues, Monograph 27. New York: International Universities Press, 1972, 213 pp., $8.50.

In order to study their problem, the authors attempted to avoid strictly laboratory conditions by investigating the effects of stressful situations occurring in group therapy and in individuals awaiting surgery. They found that "it is affect-related or emotionally arousing information of personal relevance that one dreams about." Subjects reacted to the same or similar experience in significantly different ways. Their data consistently revealed the persisting effects of early experiences, particularly the relationships within the family. "… personality characteristics are embodied in … memory systems and determine perception … (… determine the particular course of fantasy, thought, and dreams); and guide action and interchange with the environment." The REM state is one of a number which facilitate access to these memory systems. The impact of contemporary events upon these systems serves to facilitate solutions to problems brought into focus by stress, but only if the data are analyzed and interpreted. Merely dreaming does not solve problems.

NEW HORIZON FOR PSYCHOTHERAPY: AUTONOMY AS A PROFESSION. Edited by Robert R. Holt. New York: International Universities Press, 1971, 418 pp., $15.00.

The central problem of discussion in this book is whether psychotherapy should be recognized as a distinct profession, with specific standards and methods of teaching and training and recognition as an autonomous discipline, neither medical nor psychological as such, but combining the most desirable features of both. Ancillary problems taken up are: should the emphasis be on producing large numbers of psychotherapists, with necessarily less training or smaller numbers of more highly skilled ones; nature of the curriculum; use of existing facilities or establishment of entirely new ones; the question of personal analysis for trainees; training in research; etc. Different views are presented concerning these problems.

Albert Solnit  

We are more likely to keep in mind the crucial importance of motor discharge in childhood when we are treating children psychoanalytically, than we are able to include this behavior and its meaning when we are reconstructing childhood in adult analysis. The behavior of the child, and even of the younger adolescent, normatively is communicated and strongly influenced by movement, motor discharge, and physical expressive behavior. Once the child is in the latency period, we may acknowledge the importance of body-language, of motor discharge patterns, and the movements and posturing of the body, but we tend to overlook or avoid the specific meanings that can be conveyed by these behavioral characteristics and patterns.

This paper by Elaine V. Siegel may go too far in the direction of explicitness, but it is a refreshing reminder of the richness and complexity of children's behavior. Can we use such presumptions and understanding in our clinical and theoretical thinking? The ego functions of control and modulation of neuromuscular movement, of motor discharge, of posture and antigravity behavior, for example, suggest available pathways of influence that can be utilized to strengthen the ego in general through strengthening specific functions of the ego.

Siegel, in her report of how she worked with and assisted a seven-year-old girl with a school phobia, has described how she related to Debby and how she "taught" Debby to assert and practice mastery of her body behavior.

Submitted July 7, 1972

James McLaughlin  

The Board on Professional Standards and its committees: Francis McLaughlin, Chairman.

Francis McLaughlin opened the panel presentation by alluding to the current ferment in organized psychoanalysis, much of it focused upon overt issues of criteria and categories of membership in our Association. This, he felt, pointed to a growing and challenging interest in the philosophy and standards of psychoanalytic education. The place and functions of the Board on Professional Standards and its various committees in relation to these issues often seem to be not clearly understood, for reasons that include a general lack of information regarding the organization of the Association itself. The Association in its most general sense is an aggregate of individual psychoanalysts who vote for their officers and by-laws. Clustered locally or regionally into Societies, they function collectively to designate Society representatives to the Executive Council to direct and transact business pertinent to a scientific and professional organization. Clustered into faculties and psychoanalytic institutes, they designate institute representatives (two Fellows for each of the now 21 accredited institutes) to the Board on Professional Standards to direct and transact business pertinent to an educational organization. Only a fraction of the total membership is involved and represented in these institute and Board functions, and the result for the rest seems too often to have been a sense of distancing from the educational affairs of the Association and a perception of the Board as arbitrary and nonprogressive.

A Plenary Session presentation held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association New York, N. Y., December, 1972. Chairman: Francis McLaughlin, M.D.

Submitted: March 3, 1972

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editor's Introduction

Harold Blum M.D.

Harold Blum

Harold Blum  

I AM TAKING THIS OPPORTUNITY to extend greetings to colleagues and readers, and to offer some perspective and commentary.

I succeed and salute Dr. John Frosch, the founding father of this Journal, now Editor Emeritus. Dr. Frosch had not only the exceptional wisdom and foresight to establish the Journal, but the insight and expertise to guide it through an entire generation of psychoanalysis. His perceptiveness and progressive innovation were also reflected in the organization of the Editorial Board as a diverse elective and representative group of outstanding psychoanalysts. Dr. Frosch developed high standards for the Journal and an autonomous position which kept it free from political and professional pressures. Dr. Nathaniel Ross rendered distinguished service as Associate Editor. He brought to the Journal his intellectual acuity and profound analytic scholarship. The book section was especially enhanced by Dr. Ross's sensitive and critical discernment. From its inception, the Journal was most fortunate in its editorial direction and integrity. We are indebted beyond measure for the unswerving dedication and illustrious accomplishment of our retiring editors and wish them every success in their new undertakings. We look forward to their further professional and scientific contributions.

Psychoanalysis, a science of growth and development, continues its own growth and development, enlarging the understanding of personality in all its complexity. The scientific and therapeutic achievements of psychoanalysis are related to its capacity for self-scrutiny and the integration of new evidence and explanation.

The Journal is a vital part of our analytic development in promulgating psychoanalytic knowledge and principles as well as in expressing creative ferment and disparate trends. Psychoanalysis will ultimately benefit from present challenges, for challenges have elicited and provoked healthy self-criticism and expansion and reformulation of our concepts. The Journal will strive to reconcile the paradox of preserving our analytic heritage and at the same time promoting new development and constructive controversy.

On a Particular Form of Masturbation in Women: Masturation with Water

Eugene Halpert M.D.

Eugene Halpert

Eugene Halpert, M.D.
2 Parkside Drive
Great Neck, New York 11021

Eugene Halpert  

SUMMARY

Women who masturbate by running water over their clitoris are using this form of masturbation to express the supplementary fantasies: I have my father's penis and can urinate/ejaculate like a man, and I am able to urinate and destroy/castrate with my powerful stream in revenge for castration. Other subsidiary oral, anal, and phallic fantasies and memories help to determine and are expressed in this form of masturbation. Further ideas about the selection of this particular form of masturbation are put forth.

[[RunningHead]]

A Study of the Representation of Objects in Schizophrenia

Stanley Grand Ph.D. , Norbert Freedman Ph.D. and Irving Steingart Ph.D.

Irving Steingart

Stanley Grand, Ph.D.
Norbert Freedman, Ph.D.
Irving Steingart, Ph.D.
Department of Psychiatry (Box 88) State University of New York Downstate Medical Center
Brooklyn, New York 11203

Irving Steingart  

SUMMARY

A recent trend evident in the literature on schizophrenia appears to focus upon variations in the qualities of maladaptive object related behavior evident in the interpersonal functioning of schizophrenic patients. These qualities of interpersonal behavior have relevance for the understanding of intrapsychic factors in mental functioning, particularly when seen from the structural and adaptive metapsychological points of view.

In the present paper, two groups of chronic schizophrenic patients exhibiting widely differing, although highly stable forms of maladaptive object-related behavior (i.e., Isolation vs. Belligerence) were given psychological tests and seen in a clinical interview which was video-recorded. Their test- and interview behavior were studied with regard to their relative capacities to represent objects, that is, to transform experience into a verbal form. Three measures utilized in this study were the Stroop Color-Word task, a system of coding language which provides a measure of linguistic complexity in sentence construction; and a system of coding kinetic behavior which provides a measure of communicative and noncommunicative speech-related gestures.

Our results indicated that Isolated patients performed significantly more poorly on one of the linguistic encoding tasks (simple color-naming); utilized significantly more simple forms of language construction; and significantly more gestures that were

430

unrelated to speech and that appear to be related to linguistic encoding difficulties. By contrast, the Belligerent patients exhibited more conflict on the interference portion of the linguistic encoding task; used syntactically more complex forms of language construction; utilized gestures that were highly integrated with the speech flow and that appear to be related to integrative processes involved in more effective linguistic encoding.

These findings were seen to reflect the difficulty which the Isolated patients have in integrating "ideo-motor" aspects of experience. That is, Isolated patients show a deficit in the capacity to utilize verbally encoded thought to represent their experience, and this results in greater difficulty in integrating thought and action. The role of aggression and its deneutralization was discussed in connection with its possible significance in the impairment of the representational function in schizophrenia.

Adrienne Applegarth, M.D.

Adrienne Applegarth  

The extraordinarily rich substance of the volumes to be considered in this essay invites scrutiny from many angles. All of the authors have undertaken an examination of some aspect of psychoanalytic theory, yet their aims vary a good deal. The focus of some is broad and abstract, while that of others is more restricted and closer to the data of observation. Some devote themselves to explication, and some undertake to change aspects of the theory. Naturally, there are also differences among them in the degree to which they have succeeded in their attempts. We shall assay their influences upon the development of psychoanalytic theory; further questions must then be raised as to whether the theory has indeed changed, in what ways, and against what obstacles.

From Department of Psychiatry, Mt. Zion Hospital and Medical Center, San Francisco, California.

W. Meissner

W. Meissner

W. W. Meissner, S.J.
Six Sumner Road
Cambridge, Mass. 02138

W. Meissner  

SUMMARY

To summarize the crucial distinction we wish to draw between learning processes and identificatory processes, the central element in the differentiation hinges on the problem of internalization. Learning processes can directly influence and bring about representational structuralization in the subject's inner cognitive world. They can also indirectly produce functional structuralizations in a variety of functions and combinations of functions, both of ego and superego. Learning processes do not effect structuralizing internalizations which constitute the internal organization of the self. Consequently, identificatory processes which are specifically internalizing cannot be reduced to or explained by learning processes as such.

Albert Solnit explained that this was the first in a series of three panels to be jointly sponsored by the Association for Child Psychoanalysis and the American Psychoanalytic Association. The next panel—Adolescence and Maturity—is planned for the Annual Meeting in Dallas, 1972; the final panel, planned for the Fall Meeting in New York, 1972, will discuss Maturity, Senescence and Sociological Implications.

The study of object relations has been a focus of psychoanalytic attention for many years, and Solnit emphasized that Margaret Mahler's contributions have opened new horizons for the study of normal as well as pathological processes; they have led to an understanding that empathy and tenderness as a goal can be experienced by an individual in a human relationship only after he has achieved a necessary degree of separateness.

Margaret Mahler's paper, "On the First Three Phases of the Separation-Individuation Process," summed up a 12- to 15-year study of the normal separation-individuation process. This work focused on the examination of phenomena entailed in two Freudian concepts: (1) that the human being is at first absolutely and remains later on relatively dependent on a mother; (2) as a result, object relationship, i.e., one person's endowing another with object libido, is the most reliable single factor by which we are able to determine, on the one hand, the level of mental health and, on the other, the extent of the therapeutic potential.

TO BEGIN WITH I WOULD VERY MUCH LIKE to thank the American psychoanalysts for the great honor they do me, and the great pleasure they give me in inviting me to speak to you at the general congress of your celebrated association. In fact I consider this invitation as having a scientific significance which it pleases me to be able to underscore. There was a time when the relations between psychoanalysis and what your predecessors referred to (with some contempt) as academic psychology were complete misunderstandings arising from mutual neglect (to be euphemistic). Since then, the representatives of scientific psychology, who have the advantage over you of not being bound to any school, have come, with varying speed, to understand the importance of the central ideas of Freudian psychoanalysis, and have incorporated them into their general theories of conduct, again, with varying degrees of modification. Psychoanalytic Technique and the Ego's Capacity for Viewing Intrapsychic Activity

Paul Gray M.D.

Paul Gray

Paul Gray, M.D.
1616 18th st. N.W.
Washington, D.C. 2009

Paul Gray  

SUMMARY

In summary, this has been a consideration of that aspect of analysis of the neuroses concerned with the listening analyst's perspective of attention or perceptual focus. A preference has been indicated for adjusting this focus so as to observe data limited essentially to inside the analytic situation. Elaboration of this point of view included illustrating specific advantages that it offers in the analysis of communications of the patient referring to current or contemplated

492

behavior outside the analytic situation, sometimes labeled as "acting out."

The preceding have been considered in terms of the evolution of psychoanalytic technique—the increasing understanding of the role of the ego, the diminishing importance of measures of suggestion and persuasion, the increasing awareness of the importance of not pre-empting the patient's ego function during the course of the analysis.

It is suggested that the ideas that are stressed are also in the interest of improving analysands' capacities for eventual self-analysis. I believe that greater effectiveness of psychoanalytic technique in increasing the ego's autonomy lies in further discoveries about the functions with which the normal ego can perceive itself.

[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Erotized Transference

Harold Blum M.D.

Harold Blum

Harold P. Blum, M.D.
23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn Estates, New York 11576

Harold Blum  

SUMMARY

The concept of erotized transference is differentiated as a subspecies of erotic transference. Though more common in patients with impaired ego function, erotized transference is not limited solely to these patients. The erotization may recede and recur with transference vicissitudes. Analyzability depends upon resources of

74

the total personality and capacity for ego growth. The significance and meaning of erotized transference is explored, with special reference to regressive repetition of childhood seduction and masturbation, and a seductive style of defense. The analytic situation is a seduction or danger to be warded off by seduction. Defense against hostility, homosexuality, and narcissistic injury is prominent. Parental seduction and denial of complicity are models of seductive style and superego corruption. The sense of reality is frequently altered and the more severe cases display defective reality testing and object relations. Narcissistic and preoedipal problems are formative influences, sometimes with an oedipal façade. The narcissism of the "privileged character" may be expressed in insistent favoritism as an exception. Sadomasochistic exploitation may be disguised as erotic conquest. Erotized transference can also be viewed as a distorted attempt to master trauma via active repetition. In the more benign cases, oedipal passions and disappointment may be the core conflict. Erotized transference is not necessarily ego syntonic in depth, and can be anchored in countertransference responses.

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore J. Jacobs, M.D.
12 West 96th Street
New York, New York 10025

Theodore Jacobs  

SUMMARY

In this communication, I have tried to show how the analyst's own bodily responses as he listens to, or otherwise interacts with, the patient can assist him in his analytic work. By monitoring his own kinesic behavior as it occurs in response to his shifting psychological state, he may be able to put himself more finely in tune with his unconscious reactions. This increased self-awareness can then be used, either in the service of providing clues to the meaning of the patient's communications, or in facilitating the recognition of previously undetected attitudes and feelings in the analyst himself.

Psychoanalytic Perspectives on the Problem of Reality

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
Mount Zion Hospital and Medical Center
1600 Divisadero Street

Robert Wallerstein  

THE PRIVILEGE ACCORDED THE IMMEDIATE RETIRED PRESIDENT of the Association to address it in plenary session carries with it a corresponding obligation to select a suitably large topic whose scope warrants directing your attention to it, in the hope that a dialogue about it initiated or furthered here can lead to theoretical or empirical additions to our scientific understandings. Such a topic, I think, is the one I have chosen today, one relatively neglected or taken for granted in usual psychoanalytic discourse, namely the psychoanalytic study of reality. Although Freud's theoretical reformulations, in "The Ego and the Id" in 1923 and "Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety" in 1926, into the structural theory that placed the ego at the crossroads where the drive demands of the id, the moral constraints of the superego, and the requirements of the outer reality all met and were mediated, implicitly made reality directly comparable to the intrapsychic instances in its role in mental life, the full implications of this repositioning for our theory have never been adequately remarked or more than rudimentarily developed.

Historically this circumstance is readily understandable. In its beginnings and for several decades, psychoanalysis was primarily a drive psychology—the elaboration of the vicissitudes of instinctuality in all their ramifying complexity.

Chief, Department of Psychiatry, Mt. Zion Hospital and Medical Center, San Francisco. Presented in condensed form at plenary session, American Psychoanalytic Association on December 2, 1972, in New York City.

SUMMARY

The case of a patient with a narcissistic personality structure is described, and the course of his four-and-a-half years of analysis given. The nature of the narcissistic transference that underlay the transference neurosis is set forth. Evidence is offered to support the thesis that the patient used specific fantasies as though they were intangible representations of transitional objects.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Two Forms of Intimacy

William Binstock M.D.

William Binstock

William A. Binstock, M.D.
51 Brattle Street
Cambridge, Mass 02138

William Binstock  

SUMMARY

The pursuit of union, a pervasive theme in human psychology, is examined from the adaptational point of view by relating it to both oral and genital intimacy. The extreme dependency of the infant creates, through separation anxiety and consequent strivings for fusion, a lifelong threat to autonomy, and generates diverse searches for blissful union, which share a mystical character and a fear of annihilation derived from fantasies seeking to reconstruct preverbal experiences.

Development of the capacity for a mature heterosexual relationship (i.e., achievement of a genital object representation) constitutes the most valuable protection from this threat to autonomy. The actual enjoyment of such a relationship continuously refines,

105

extends, and enriches the autonomy of the individual lovers involved. Crucial to this solution of an adaptational dilemma is the resolution of bisexuality through the focus of lovers upon a reciprocal differentiation into male and female. In marked contrast to all other relationships (real or fantasied), closeness to a heterosexual lover promotes a secure sense of autonomy rather than threatens it—no amount of physical and emotional intermingling can overthrow the paramount sense that one is the "he" and one the "she."

Other topics discussed include sexual reality, dependency, activity and passivity, pleasure, infatuation, love, and the role of continuous sexual activity in differentiating man from the animals.

SUMMARY

It is apparent from the foregoing that the assessment of suitability for analysis is a complex task. This protocol has been formlated in an attempt to present a well-constructed system for evaluation which can be used by colleagues in the project of developing and sharing experience concerning analyzability. The particular advantage of this profile is that it may be used with a limited number of interviews, without compromising systematic scrutiny of personality functions relevant to analyzability. It also provides an assessment scale which will allow comparisons of the same person at various points in time and of different patients rated reliably within the same scale. This profile represents the first step in the practical and theoretical systematization of analyzability at the Washington Psychoanalytic Institute. It is hoped that it will stimulate others to join in such study.

[[RunningHead]]

Notes on the Concept of Splitting and the Defense Mechanism of the Splitting of Representations

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D. and Joseph Slap M.D.

Joseph Slap

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
Joseph William Slap, M.D.
1616 18th Street, N. W.
Washington, D. C. 20009

Joseph Slap  

SUMMARY

We have considered the varied and confusing meanings of the term "splitting" under four headings: splitting as a general organizing principle, splitting and the organizing of mental contents in infantile life, the defense mechanism of splitting of representations,

785

and splitting of representations as a factor in pathological intersystemic suborganizations.

We have suggested that Hartmann's concepts of differentiation and integration, as a general organizational principle of psychic structure formation (the counterpart of synthesis), are superior to that of splitting. Also that the conception of "pathological intersystemic suborganizations" is a more precise designation for contradictory coexistent psychical groupings, as exemplified by the fetishist, than is the term "splitting of the ego."

We would restrict the term "splitting" to the tendency in infantile life by which the organization of memory traces of the earliest experiences is based on the primordial quality of pleasurable-good or painful-bad. By the term "splitting of representations," we would designate a mechanism of defense which acts to separate the representations of self and of objects in instances in which two differing currents of strong feelings and urges would arouse anxiety if experienced simultaneously toward an object. The one affective current, and the self-representation related to it, is experienced toward one object while the opposite current is linked to a separate object representation.

The Experience of Separation-Individuation in Infancy and its Reverberations Through the Course of Life—2. Adolescence and Maturity

Irwin Marcus M.D.

Irwin Marcus

Irwin M. Marcus, M.D.
3619 Prytania Street
New Orleans, La. 70115

Irwin Marcus  

E. James Anthony preceded his opening comments by introducing Albert J. Solnit, the chairman of the first panel in this series of three on the title theme. Solnit concisely referred to the previous panel on Infancy and Childhood and paid tribute to Margaret Mahler's contributions to our understanding of this early phase in development. Anthony then introduced the panel and noted with pleasure that in all three presentations, theory and practice are interwoven. He indicated that in his experience as an analyst he is always sensible of some refinement of individuation proceeding in himself as he analyzes, and that one's identity as an analyst undergoes many vicissitudes over the years of practice. He felt sure that the different facets of theory presented by this panel would enrich our view of this significant developmental period.

This systematic analytic examination of the life cycle should help to pool a great deal of knowledge generally kept partitioned. Moreover, it has two further advantages: it enables analysts to look at a particular process, e.g., separation-individuation, against the background of different segments of developmental time, as well as to scrutinize a particular stage of development, e.g., adolescence, in terms of the process. These two perspectives can fruitfully complement each other, but there are some built-in problems to be considered. A developmental phase, such as adolescence, with its hyperfluidity, might not provide the optimal time for investigating phenomena in depth.

Held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Dallas, May, 1972. Chairman: E. James Anthony, M.D.

Submitted November 14, 1972

Jerome Oremland M.D.

Jerome Oremland

Jerome D. Oremland, M.D.
2299 Post Street, Suite 311
San Francisco, California 94115

Jerome Oremland  

SUMMARY

Because of the unusual plasticity of dreams—their use of visual imagery and condensation—they often represent with striking clarity

300

the two main aspects of the analysis: the major symptom complex and the transference. In three cases considered successfully analyzed, a dream occurred during the termination process with the following specific characteristics: (1) it occurred in relationship to a termination event; (2) it portrayed the presenting or a major symptom with significant modification; and (3) it represented the analyst undisguised and in intimate relationship with the symptom. It is suggested that in these cases the dream demonstrates to varying degrees the alteration which the analysis has brought about in both the symptom and the transference. The symptom is changed, and the analyst's being represented in a realistic, role-appropriate manner indicates the degree to which the transference distortions have been analyzed and resolved. A fourth case, also with a termination dream, but an analysis considered incomplete, is presented for contrast.

The termination phase as a test of the analysis is discussed. It is proposed that a dream with the characteristics described, when present, is important additional evidence that an analytic process was initiated, that the symptom structure has been modified, that there has been significant resolution of the transference, and that a completing process is underway.

[[RunningHead]]

The Nonreporting Training Analyst, the Analysis, and the Institute

James McLaughlin M.D.

James McLaughlin

James T. McLaughlin, M.D.
121 University Place
Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania 15213

James McLaughlin  

SUMMARY

1. The "syncretistic dilemma" of the training analysis subsumes intrapsychic, interpersonal, and group phenomena of wide range and complexity, and much in need of ongoing clarification, since their traditional acceptance as inseparable and inevitable has too often led to a justifying of training analytic practices that would be quite unacceptable in a therapeutic analysis.

2. One such institutionalized practice, the active contribution of the training analyst to the administrative deliberations and decisions of his institute regarding the progression in training of his own analysand, is challenged in this paper.

3. The challenge lies in describing the alternative mode of the

Nonreporting Training Analyst and its effect, in actual practice, upon the training analyst and his analysand, upon the training analysis, and upon the pedagogic and administrative usages of the institute.

4. The NRTA stance is shown to provide an analytic situation closer to that of the therapeutic analysis in its avoidance of actions and attitudes which, through reporting, give reality reinforcement and gratification to a gamut of transference expectations and fantasy. Yet it permits adequate analytic attention, within the analytic situation, to the dynamics of the special differences of the training analysis.

5. The NRTA stance provides an optimal opportunity for the analysand and analyst to perceive issues of dependency and identification, passivity and rebellion, in a context open to fuller realization of the growth or autonomy and individuation in the analysand.

6. The NRTA and his stance influence and stimulate the institute and its faculty toward improved concepts and processes of pedagogy and administration, especially as these relate to candidate assessment and learning. Such processes strikingly diminish the need for and reliance upon the opinion of the training analyst about his own analysand.

7. Given the support of these enhancements in the operational processes of the institute, the NRTA stance permits a reduction in the need and opportunity for countertransference enactment, and the NRTA can comfortably maintain and monitor his stance even in the face of very difficult candidate problems.

8. The liabilities and limitations of the NRTA stance are acknowledged. It is most sorely strained and most likely to be compromised in the infrequent instance of severe psychopathy. It does not resolve all the problems and special differences of the training analysis, such as those connected with a shared professional identity and expectation of postanalytic real involvement, or those connected with the handling of extra-analytically acquired information. But the NRTA holds great advantages for most candidate analysis, and, in any event, allows a clearer perspective from which to assess those residual contaminants and special differences of the training analysis which are perhaps truly inevitable.

Martin Wangh, Chairman of the Panel, opened with a brief discussion of Freud's concept of narcissism as that portion of the libido which remains self-directed throughout life. According to Brenner, the term "pathological narcissism" includes hypercathexis of the self, hypocathexis of the object, and a pathologically immature relationship to the object. Annie Reich discussed cases of narcissistic neurosis standing midway between neurosis and psychosis. Wangh suggested that Kohut's concept of "self-object" now provides a bridge between normalcy, neurosis, and borderline and psychotic states. He noted that the reported increase in narcissistic disorders may reflect problems inherent in trends of anonymization in our families and in society at large. It is the very failure of healthy narcissistic development that seems to lead to "hypercathexis of the self" in later life. Wangh then discussed resistances to dealing with studies of narcissism. Geared to view self-centeredness as undesirable, we need to work through this resistance. Wangh expressed the hope that this Panel would contribute to that working-through effort.

Arnold Goldberg presented the first paper. Reviewing Kohut's position, Goldberg emphasized that narcissism is part of normal development, having primitive and mature forms as well as pathological ones. The narcissistic object, based on Freud's concept in his 1914 paper, is modeled upon an aspect of the self, performs a function for the self, or serves as a self-regulator. Development proceeds via the narcissistic object. Early stages preceding a coherent sense of self are postulated, in which mental and physical experiences are not yet integrated. Cohesion of the self thus depends upon a narcissistic object. Freud's description of the stage of autoerotism followed by a stage of narcissism was a

THIS STUDY OF A PROBLEM in the domain of psychic energy is undertaken with the knowledge that serious difficulties exist in further clarifying energy theory (Holt, 1967); (Schafer, 1968); (Applegarth, 1971). While some analysts have gone so far as to wish to do away with the concept of psychic energy (Klein, 1967); (Rubinstein, 1967; and others) a large majority (Lustman, 1969); (Engel, 1971) have found that much psychoanalytic data—clinical, observational, and theoretical—cannot be explained satisfactorily without such a concept. The present effort reflects my conviction that further clarification of instinctual drive theory can shed further light on the concept of psychic energy.

Whereas we know much about instinctual drive theory, some unclarity still remains, and some dissatisfaction with certain specific theoretical points, one of which I explore here. The observations that drew my attention to the problem at hand come from the Early Child Development Project, "An Epigenesis of Aggression in Early Childhood," a project10

[[RunningHead]]

Limitations of Method in Psychoanalytic Biography: A Historical Inquiry

Martin Bergmann

Martin Bergmann

Martin S. Bergmann
1136 Fifth Avenue
New York, New York 10028

Martin Bergmann  

SUMMARY

The historical development of psychoanalytic biography has been traced. It seemed useful to look upon this development as exemplifying general rules of scientific growth in terms of Kuhn's (1962) paradigm. The interest and investment of energy in psychoanalytic biography stemmed from the hope that psychoanalysis would be capable of understanding extraordinary achievement as it illuminated the riddle of neurosis. Lacking free associations, the basic tool of the clinical psychoanalyst, interpretation by symbols was

847

extensively used. This was possible because the majority of subjects were artists and therefore users of symbols. But the interpretation by symbols alone proved hazardous. Artists whose lives were dominated by an early trauma were easier to understand and therefore preferred as subjects. Psychoanalytic biographers found unique substitutes for free associations. Psychoanalytic ego psychology modified the basic model by emphasizing the ego as well as the unconscious wishes. It also stressed developmental phases other than early infancy. We may be witnessing the emergence of a new model.

Conversion Hysteria and Conversion Symptoms: A Revision of Classification and Concepts

Melitta Sperling M.D.

Melitta Sperling

Melitta Sperling, M.D.
960 Park Avenue
New York, New York 10028

Melitta Sperling  

ALTHOUGH CONVERSION HYSTERIA was the first neurosis to be studied psychoanalytically, much about hysteria and conversion has remained problematic. While it is true that cases of classical conversion hysteria are infrequent nowadays, especially among the more sophisticated segments of the population with which analysts usually deal, conversion hysteria and conversion symptoms are nevertheless still fairly common neurotic phenomena. In the pediatric department of a general hospital, I have seen classical cases of conversion hysteria such as paralysis of extremities, hysterical blindness, hysterical (epileptiform) convulsions. In my 30 years of analytic practice, I have treated a considerable number of patients with true conversion hysteria and a large number of patients with conversion symptoms in all age groups.

Among my patients was one whose symptomatology in many ways resembled that of Freud's Dora case. I have previously described this patient (Sperling, 1953) with specific reference to her food allergies. My focus here will be broader, for I wish to show how her early traumata influenced the shaping of her fantasy life and her choice of symptoms.

Mrs. A.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, State University of New York, Downstate Medical Center, Division of Psychoanalytic Education.

Submitted February 21, 1973

Robert Hatcher Ph.D.

Robert Hatcher

Robert L. Hatcher, Ph.D.
Department of Psychiatry University of Michigan Medical Center
Ann Arbor, Mich. 48104

Robert Hatcher  

SUMMARY

Insight has been a key tool in psychoanalytic treatment since its earliest days. Analytic thought has come to view the acquisition of insight as a complex process, in which the ego function of self-observation is a major component. After tracing the evolution of this line of thought, detailed consideration is given to self-observation, its nature, its structure, and its place in the analytic process.

THE MAIN PART of Sigmund Freud's library is preserved at the home of his daughter, Anna Freud, in London. When Freud left Vienna in June, 1938 in the face of the German occupation, he was permitted to take his personal effects, including his collection of antiquities and books. The Nazis initially threatened to confiscate the library, relenting only upon being paid over $800 in ransom money (Jones, 1957). During the last few weeks in Vienna, while awaiting permission to leave, Freud went through his library, selected those books he wished to take to London, and disposed of the others. This last group—described below—was eventually acquired by the New York State Psychiatric Institute.

The library in London provides a valuable key to the range of Freud's reading. It includes source material not available in present bibliographies of Freud's published writings, and thus it provides evidence for Freud's familiarity with heretofore presumptive or unsuspected antecedents. In addition, the present composition of the library is a product of Freud's judgment in his later years of books he thought useful or worthy of preservation.

From the time Freud became a student he had a passion for collecting and owning books. He alludes to the sources of this passion in his analysis of the dream of the botanical monograph

The authors would like to express their appreciation to Anna Freud for putting the full resources of the Freud Library at their disposal; to Gertrud Dann, Librarian, Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic, for verifying doubtful items in the catalogue; to James W. Montgomery, Associate Librarian, New York State Psychiatric Institute, for helping with the Freud Library in New York; to Howard W. Winger, of the Graduate Library School, University of Chicago, for many helpful suggestions; Gloria Simmons for several proofreadings and corrections; and to Paula Fichtl, the Freud housekeeper, who reconciled the senior author to his labors with delicious English tea and Viennese pastry.

In order to afford a provisional structure for the discussion, Arnold Pfeffer, Chairman of the Panel, began by posing the following questions: what remains of the transference and transference neurosis after even the most ideally completed analysis? What aspects of the analyst, his interpretations, and all else that goes on in an analysis afterward continue to be psychically represented? What is the nature and function of such remainders in the analytic and therapeutic gains? Do internalized transference conflicts and their resolutions become central elements in the structural changes for which we aim? More particularly, does the psychic representation of the analyst and his interpretations forever continue to play a vital role in the maintenance of such resolutions? If so, how does the analyst develop this important and enduring meaning in the course of the analysis?

Pfeffer has observed that in informal discussions with colleagues on these matters a great deal of interest inevitably is stimulated, and that the responses tend to take the form of subjective, conscious recollections of their analyses. Memories can sometimes be isolated and screenlike, such as those that involve aspects of their analyst's office decorations, the color of the analyst's hair, etc., or, probably more rarely—as occurred in one instance—they can encompass in considerable detail the entire analysis. Joan Fleming, the chairman, initiated the panel proceedings by calling attention to the innovation of a continuity of theme at three successive panel meetings. This arrangement has made it possible to dynamically correlate observed behavior at different stages of the life cycle with theoretical concepts. The collaboration helps to undo the isolation in our thinking that is epitomized in the semantic "identity confusion" of the terms "child analyst" and "adult analyst." This Panel, the third, focused on the influence of separation-individuation as a process on the periods of maturity and senescence in an attempt to compare the impact on these periods of life with the earlier periods of childhood, adolescence, and youthful adulthood.

Albert J. Solnit, chairman of the panel dealing with infancy and childhood and the separation-individuation process, expected that the differing senses of time of the adult, the adolescent, and the child would be reflected in the panel's discussions. Time moves rapidly for the adult. The slower movement for the child is demonstrated by his poor tolerance of separation. Separateness causes discomfort because of the child's limited ability to hold on to the mental representation of the adult love-object for very long while the love object is absent. Individuation, on the other hand, proceeds more rapidly for the child, whereas the periodic review that advances individuation for the adult occurs slowly.

Solnit added that a continuity of affectionate bonds is crucial for the achievement of autonomy by the child. This sense of continuity is also essential for learning, memory, adaptation, planning and preparing for the future, and to give meaning to life—regardless of cultural differences.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 3, 1972, Chairman: Joan Fleming, M.D.

Submitted: March 31, 1973

George H. Pollock, M.D.

George Pollock  

BERTHA PAPPENHEIM CONTINUES TO INTEREST psychoanalysts and other researchers even though her contacts with Breuer ended in 1882—some 90 years ago. Since the preparation of my recently published paper on Bertha Pappenheim (1972), Lucy Freeman's carefully researched book on Anna O. (1972), and Ellenberger's article (1972) containing new information about post-Breuer Bertha Pappenheim have appeared.

In the following paragraphs, I should like to focus on Ellenberger's data, which fill in some gaps in Bertha's life and which seem consistent with my offered explanation about Bertha's pathological mourning and its significance in her pathology.

Ellenberger's constant efforts to obtain information about Bertha Pappenheim, especially in the period following her therapy with Breuer, are, I feel, noteworthy. Karpe tells us that despite the termination of Bertha's treatment with Breuer in June 1882, she was subsequently hospitalized in a sanatarium; that in 1887, Martha Freud reported that Miss Pappenheim "felt pretty well during the days but … she still suffered from her hallucinatory states in the evenings" (Karpe, 1961). In 1890, she published a collection of grief stories, In der Trödelbude, under the pseudonym of Paul Berthold. In 1895, she became the house-mother in a Frankfurt Jewish orphanage, and from then on her career in social work was launched. The many achievements of her later adult life, without apparent illness, are well known.

Three Letters from Sigmund Freud to André Breton

Frederick Davis M.D.

Frederick Davis

Frederick B. Davis, M.D.
Division of Child Psychiatry Univ. of Wash. School of Medicine
Seattle, Washington 98105

Frederick Davis  

PSYCHOANALYSIS, ORIGINATING IN VIENNA and in the mind of a single brilliant man, has come to have worldwide recognition (if not acceptance) and has influenced the development of concepts in such diverse fields as anthropology, art, history, sociology, literary criticism, and law. One of the clearest examples of such an effect may be found in the work of André Breton, the founder of Surrealism.

In 1916 Breton served as a medical aide in the psychiatric center at Saint-Dizier where he came in contact with mental patients evacuated from the front. It was here that he first heard of psychoanalysis and began to read Freud's works. He recorded patients' hallucinations and delusional experiences and subsequently tried to "interpret" them, relying on psychoanalytic principles. After the war, Breton along with other Dadaists experimented with what came to be known as automatic writing. This consisted of writing rapidly without pausing to reread or correct, or writing while in self-induced trance states, or recording the hypnogogic phenomena that occur just before sleep. Several years later, by then the acknowledged leader of the Surrealists, Breton utilized actual dream material in his poetry and lyrical "interpretations." Some of the early Surrealist works are recordings of the dreams of artists and writers, and for a time there was actually an agency in Paris for the collection of dreams from the populace at large.

Breton first corresponded with Freud in 1919, and paid him a visit in 1921.

Beating Fantasies and Daydreams Revisited: Presentation of a Case

Winslow Hunt M.D.

Winslow Hunt

Winslow Hunt, M.D.
336 Central Park West
New York, N.Y. 10025

Winslow Hunt  

SUMMARY

A case history is presented in which masochistic fantasies of two kinds, crude beating fantasies and refined elaborate daydreams, played a central role in the patient's life and in her analysis. Study of this case and review of the psychoanalytic literature indicate that the central meaning of such fantasies is the wish to be loved by the father. Such fantasies develop in childhood in a setting of sibling rivalry and can be crystallized by a single traumatic experience. In adult life, such fantasies contain themes of reproach and accusation, are kept isolated from the rest of the mental life, and appear in response to current experiences of deprivation.

The elaborate, masochistic daydream which is associated with beating fantasies has in the past been considered to be an attempt to obtain the libidinal gratification of a beating fantasy while still avoiding masturbation. Analysis of this case suggests that the crude beating fantasy and the associated refined daydream both express the wish to be loved by the father, but the beating fantasy itself

831

expresses the wish for a sexual act with the father, while the daydream expresses the narcissistic aspect of the wish for the father's love—that is, the desire for the father's love in order to create an inner image of the self as good, attractive, and lovable.

Original Sin: "I Didn't Mean to Hurt You, Mother"—A Basic Fantasy Epitomized by a Male Homosexual

Irving Berent M.D.

Irving Berent

Irving Berent, M.D.
16500 Ventura Blvd.
Encino, California 91316

Irving Berent  

MY PREMISE IS THAT CERTAIN INDIVIDUALS develop a myth or fantasy that their own births have caused never-to-be-forgotten pain in their mothers; and among their attempts at reparation, they vow that they will never again be the cause of comparable damage to their mothers or subsequent equivalents—namely, all other women. Therefore, in the extreme, there is to be no sexual intercourse with women. No pregnancy. No childbirth. I believe such a myth (independent of the important factors that may have condensed and coalesced to produce it—overprotective mothers, absent fathers, excess castration anxiety, and the like) develops a life of its own and further cripples the individual, much as the phobic patient may be progressively crippled by his malignant "cancerous" phobia, no matter how or with what factors it was produced.

To illustrate this "myth," I shall concentrate on a series of dreams of a bisexual male seen in analysis, who told the majority of them to me during a two-month period following an hour in which he made a remarkable error that revealed a link between his observations of events surrounding his sister's birth, when he was six years old, and his circumcision. This condensation of events, the narration of which occurred after he had been in treatment for approximately a year, was revealed by his stating that he was six years old at the time of circumcision when, in fact, some months previously he had told the therapist he was 12 years old.

Submitted June 6, 1972

SUMMARY

The movement and dance therapist searches for the somatic expressions of felt experiences, both in the muscular tensions and locomotor behavior of the patient, in order to help him recreate homeostasis between psyche and soma—that delicate balance in which action and thought function interdependently and smoothly under the direction of a well-defined ego.

Book Notices

FOCAL PSYCHOTHERAPY: AN EXAMPLE OF APPLIED PSYCHOANALYSIS. By Michael Balint, Paul H. Ornstein, and Enid Balint. London: Tavistock Publications, 1972, 166 pp., $10.00.

This book evaluates the techniques and processes of "focal" psychotherapy by means of a detailed description of the interaction between patient and therapist throughout the treatment and follow-up of one patient. The concept of focal psychotherapy was developed by Michael Balint in his work on shorter forms of treatment using psychoanalytic technique. It consists in the choice of a single focus on which the treatment may be concentrated.

The substance of the book is Chapter V, which illustrates the application of this technique to the successful treatment of jealousy paranoia in a 43-year-old man who was seen over a period of 15 months for a total of 27 sessions. It also contains two follow-up sessions as well as letters and reports from the general practitioner up to six years later, which give evidence of the patient's maintenance and continued gain of the analytic insight he acquired during his treatment. The chapter is remarkable in that it consists of the individual treatment hours dictated by Michael Balint at the end of each session, classified according to what the therapist expected; the atmosphere of the session as contributed by the patient and the doctor; the main trends of the session; the interpretations that were thought of but not given; what happened to the focal aim; the outcome; and afterthoughts. All this affords the reader a fascinating view of, not only the surgical precision with which the technique is applied, but also the unspoken thoughts, including the doubts and puzzlements of the therapist. It also enables the reader to follow the red thread of the chosen focus as it runs through, regardless of time interval, from one session to the next. This section also contains the psychological test results on two separate occasions in the course of the treatment, and the above noted follow-up.
